title: Bradycardia — symptomatic
oneLiner: Symptomatic bradycardia, HR <60 with hypotension/dizziness/syncope, usually due to AV block, sick sinus, drug effect, or MI.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **High-grade AV block (Mobitz II / 3rd degree) + haemodynamic instability** — look for wide QRS escape (<40 bpm), hypotension (SBP <90), or pre-syncope. Needs immediate pacing.
- **Massive inferior STEMI with heart block** — bradycardia + ST elevation II, III, aVF + hypotension ± RV involvement (ST elevation in V4R). Thrombolysis/pacing ± fluids before PCI.
- **Hyperkalaemia (ECG: tall peaked T waves, widened QRS, sine wave)** — bradycardia here is terminal; treat with IV calcium gluconate, insulin/dextrose, nebulised salbutamol.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Pre-syncope, syncope, exertional dizziness, fatigue, breathlessness on minimal exertion, chest discomfort
- **Key exam findings:** HR <50 (often 30s-40s); hypotension (SBP <100); cool peripheries; delayed capillary refill; raised JVP with cannon waves (complete heart block); variable intensity of S1 (atrial kick); may have signs of heart failure (basal crackles, peripheral oedema)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Sinus bradycardia** — asymptomatic (most people) / increased vagal tone (athletes, vomiting, suction) — HR often 40–50, narrow QRS, normal BP, symptoms minimal
- **AV block (1st degree)** — PR >200 ms, no haemodynamic effect unless very long
- **Mobitz I (Wenckebach)** — gradually increasing PR, then dropped QRS; rarely symptomatic
- **Mobitz II / 3rd degree AV block** — sudden dropped QRS or complete AV dissociation; often P waves without relationship; needs pacing
- **Sick sinus syndrome** — alternating bradycardia (sinus pause >3 s) and tachyarrhythmia; more in elderly
- **Drug-induced** (beta-blockers, verapamil/diltiazem, digoxin, amiodarone, clonidine) — check drug chart
- **Hypothyroidism** — slow HR + weight gain, cold intolerance, coarse skin, reflex delay
- **Head injury / raised ICP** — Cushing reflex (bradycardia + hypertension + irregular breathing) — emergency CT head
- **Splitting clue:** Wide QRS escape rhythm (≥120 ms) → high-grade AV block with ventricular escape, requires pacing; narrow QRS escape → junctional rhythm, may be transient (e.g. vagal).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):** Continuous ECG monitoring; 12-lead ECG (look for P waves, PR/QRS duration, AV dissociation); BP lying/standing; rhythm strip to document pause or block; check NEWS2.
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, Mg, Ca, CRP, troponin (hs-cTnI/T), TFT, digoxin level (if on digoxin).
- **Imaging:** CXR (if heart failure suspected, cardiomegaly, lung congestion). Echo if structural heart disease suspected (but not in acute ward – request as inpatient if needed).
- **Special tests:** Carotid sinus massage (only after ruling out carotid bruit, ideally with ECG monitoring) – to diagnose carotid sinus hypersensitivity.
- **Never-miss test:** ECG – every patient with syncope/presyncope must have a 12-lead ECG. Also check drug chart for rate-slowing medications.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED/ward):**
    - ABC stabilisation. IV access – large bore. Monitor non-invasive BP, SpO2.
    - If hypotensive (SBP <90) or unstable (syncope, chest pain, heart failure signs): **Atropine 0.5 mg IV** (repeat every 3-5 min up to 3 mg total).
    - If no response to atropine: **transcutaneous pacing** – apply pads, set rate 60–70 bpm; start at low output (mA) and increase until capture (pacer spike followed by QRS). Use sedation (midazolam or morphine) as per local policy for awake patient.
    - If pacing not available or fails: IV adrenaline (epinephrine) 2–10 mcg/min or IV isoprenaline [check local policy – often not first-line due to proarrhythmia].
    - Treat underlying cause: withhold any causal drugs (beta-blockers, CCBs, digoxin); give IV calcium gluconate if hyperkalemia; treat MI (aspirin, ticagrelor, PCI).
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
    - Continuous telemetry.
    - 12-lead ECG daily or with any change.
    - Check electrolytes (K+, Mg, Ca) and correct; check thyroid function if low.
    - Consider temporary transvenous pacing if high-grade block persists or recurrent haemodynamic instability.
    - Refer to cardiology for permanent pacemaker decision (usually for symptomatic 2nd or 3rd degree AV block, sick sinus with pauses >3 s).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - If pacemaker inserted: follow local device clinic follow-up.
    - If reversible cause (drug, hyperkalaemia, hypothyroidism): monitor off drugs or after correction; repeat 24h tape or loop recorder if symptoms recur.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% saline 500 mL bolus if hypotensive (unless signs of heart failure), then maintenance 1 mL/kg/h 0.9% saline.
    - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin (standard dose) unless contraindicated (active bleeding, weight extremes; check local protocol).
    - **Diet/NBM:** Usually normal diet, but NBM if planning transvenous pacing.
    - **Observations:** NEWS2 every 30 min until stable, then 4-hourly. Continuous ECG (telemetry) – escalation if pauses >5 s or HR<30.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agent: Atropine sulfate** 0.5 mg IV bolus every 3–5 min; maximum total 3 mg. Duration: effects last ~30 min. Contraindications: narrow-angle glaucoma, myasthenia gravis (relative), prostatic hypertrophy (can cause retention) – but in emergency, use anyway.
- **Alternative: Glycopyrrolate** 200–400 mcg IV (less tachycardia and fewer central side effects) [check local policy if not in your formulary].
- **Pacing sedation:** Midazolam 1–2 mg IV (slowly over 2 min) for awake patient during transcutaneous pacing; or morphine 2–5 mg IV.
- **For underlying heart block (Mobitz II/3rd, symptomatic):** referral for pacemaker – no drug maintenance.
- **Key interactions:** Atropine – enhances vagolytic effect; with other anticholinergics add toxicity.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Atropine is mainly metabolised by liver (CYP3A4) – no dose adjustment in mild impairment; caution in severe hepatic disease.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Atropine – giving a dose <0.5 mg IV can cause paradoxical bradycardia. Always give full 0.5 mg.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Hypotension, syncope, cardiac arrest (P-wave arrest, asystole), heart failure, myocardial ischaemia (in settings of low cardiac output)
- **Chronic:** Recurrent falls, fractures, cognitive decline; pacemaker complications (pneumothorax, lead displacement, infection)
sections.Prognosis:
- **High-grade AV block (Mobitz II, 3rd degree) with wide QRS escape** – mortality 20–30% in first year without pacing; with permanent pacing, near-normal life expectancy if no structural heart disease.
- **Sinus node dysfunction** – good prognosis with pacing; symptoms resolve, but high risk of atrial arrhythmias later.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always get a 12-lead ECG** within minutes of bradycardia with symptoms – look for AV dissociation, P-wave axis, QRS width.
- **Apply pacing pads early** – even if you think atropine might work; prepare for deterioration.
- **Check the drug chart and TFT** – a simple medication review can prevent unnecessary pacing.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give atropine in narrow-angle glaucoma or high-grade AV block with wide QRS escape** – it is often ineffective and may worsen myocardial oxygen demand.
- **Do not delay transcutaneous pacing** in a patient with hypotension and wide QRS bradycardia – time lost worsens outcome.
- **Avoid verapamil or diltiazem** for atrial tachyarrhythmias if there is underlying bradycardia – they can cause complete heart block.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** New syncope, pre-syncope or blackouts; chest pain; severe dizziness or near-fainting; palpitations.
- **Follow-up:** Cardiology outpatient appointment in 2 weeks; 24-hour Holter or event monitor if e.g. sick sinus syndrome suspected; repeat ECG at follow-up.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / arrest team if:** HR <40 with hypotension (SBP <90) despite atropine; new wide QRS bradycardia; prolonged pause >5 s on monitor; syncope witnessed; NEWS2 ≥6 or any single red (e.g. resp rate ≤5, SpO2 <85, SBP <80).
- **Specific thresholds:** GCS <13, lactate >4 mmol/L, pH <7.2, hypotension (SBP <90) not corrected by initial atropine/pacing.
- **Always inform cardiology registrar** of any new high-grade AV block – they may need to come for transvenous pacing.

rawMarkdown:
## Bradycardia — symptomatic
**One-liner:** Symptomatic bradycardia, HR <60 with hypotension/dizziness/syncope, usually due to AV block, sick sinus, drug effect, or MI.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **High-grade AV block (Mobitz II / 3rd degree) + haemodynamic instability** — look for wide QRS escape (<40 bpm), hypotension (SBP <90), or pre-syncope. Needs immediate pacing.
- **Massive inferior STEMI with heart block** — bradycardia + ST elevation II, III, aVF + hypotension ± RV involvement (ST elevation in V4R). Thrombolysis/pacing ± fluids before PCI.
- **Hyperkalaemia (ECG: tall peaked T waves, widened QRS, sine wave)** — bradycardia here is terminal; treat with IV calcium gluconate, insulin/dextrose, nebulised salbutamol.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Pre-syncope, syncope, exertional dizziness, fatigue, breathlessness on minimal exertion, chest discomfort
- **Key exam findings:** HR <50 (often 30s-40s); hypotension (SBP <100); cool peripheries; delayed capillary refill; raised JVP with cannon waves (complete heart block); variable intensity of S1 (atrial kick); may have signs of heart failure (basal crackles, peripheral oedema)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Sinus bradycardia** — asymptomatic (most people) / increased vagal tone (athletes, vomiting, suction) — HR often 40–50, narrow QRS, normal BP, symptoms minimal
- **AV block (1st degree)** — PR >200 ms, no haemodynamic effect unless very long
- **Mobitz I (Wenckebach)** — gradually increasing PR, then dropped QRS; rarely symptomatic
- **Mobitz II / 3rd degree AV block** — sudden dropped QRS or complete AV dissociation; often P waves without relationship; needs pacing
- **Sick sinus syndrome** — alternating bradycardia (sinus pause >3 s) and tachyarrhythmia; more in elderly
- **Drug-induced** (beta-blockers, verapamil/diltiazem, digoxin, amiodarone, clonidine) — check drug chart
- **Hypothyroidism** — slow HR + weight gain, cold intolerance, coarse skin, reflex delay
- **Head injury / raised ICP** — Cushing reflex (bradycardia + hypertension + irregular breathing) — emergency CT head
- **Splitting clue:** Wide QRS escape rhythm (≥120 ms) → high-grade AV block with ventricular escape, requires pacing; narrow QRS escape → junctional rhythm, may be transient (e.g. vagal).

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):** Continuous ECG monitoring; 12-lead ECG (look for P waves, PR/QRS duration, AV dissociation); BP lying/standing; rhythm strip to document pause or block; check NEWS2.
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, Mg, Ca, CRP, troponin (hs-cTnI/T), TFT, digoxin level (if on digoxin).
- **Imaging:** CXR (if heart failure suspected, cardiomegaly, lung congestion). Echo if structural heart disease suspected (but not in acute ward – request as inpatient if needed).
- **Special tests:** Carotid sinus massage (only after ruling out carotid bruit, ideally with ECG monitoring) – to diagnose carotid sinus hypersensitivity.
- **Never-miss test:** ECG – every patient with syncope/presyncope must have a 12-lead ECG. Also check drug chart for rate-slowing medications.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED/ward):**
    - ABC stabilisation. IV access – large bore. Monitor non-invasive BP, SpO2.
    - If hypotensive (SBP <90) or unstable (syncope, chest pain, heart failure signs): **Atropine 0.5 mg IV** (repeat every 3-5 min up to 3 mg total).
    - If no response to atropine: **transcutaneous pacing** – apply pads, set rate 60–70 bpm; start at low output (mA) and increase until capture (pacer spike followed by QRS). Use sedation (midazolam or morphine) as per local policy for awake patient.
    - If pacing not available or fails: IV adrenaline (epinephrine) 2–10 mcg/min or IV isoprenaline [check local policy – often not first-line due to proarrhythmia].
    - Treat underlying cause: withhold any causal drugs (beta-blockers, CCBs, digoxin); give IV calcium gluconate if hyperkalemia; treat MI (aspirin, ticagrelor, PCI).
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
    - Continuous telemetry.
    - 12-lead ECG daily or with any change.
    - Check electrolytes (K+, Mg, Ca) and correct; check thyroid function if low.
    - Consider temporary transvenous pacing if high-grade block persists or recurrent haemodynamic instability.
    - Refer to cardiology for permanent pacemaker decision (usually for symptomatic 2nd or 3rd degree AV block, sick sinus with pauses >3 s).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - If pacemaker inserted: follow local device clinic follow-up.
    - If reversible cause (drug, hyperkalaemia, hypothyroidism): monitor off drugs or after correction; repeat 24h tape or loop recorder if symptoms recur.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% saline 500 mL bolus if hypotensive (unless signs of heart failure), then maintenance 1 mL/kg/h 0.9% saline.
    - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin (standard dose) unless contraindicated (active bleeding, weight extremes; check local protocol).
    - **Diet/NBM:** Usually normal diet, but NBM if planning transvenous pacing.
    - **Observations:** NEWS2 every 30 min until stable, then 4-hourly. Continuous ECG (telemetry) – escalation if pauses >5 s or HR<30.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agent: Atropine sulfate** 0.5 mg IV bolus every 3–5 min; maximum total 3 mg. Duration: effects last ~30 min. Contraindications: narrow-angle glaucoma, myasthenia gravis (relative), prostatic hypertrophy (can cause retention) – but in emergency, use anyway.
- **Alternative: Glycopyrrolate** 200–400 mcg IV (less tachycardia and fewer central side effects) [check local policy if not in your formulary].
- **Pacing sedation:** Midazolam 1–2 mg IV (slowly over 2 min) for awake patient during transcutaneous pacing; or morphine 2–5 mg IV.
- **For underlying heart block (Mobitz II/3rd, symptomatic):** referral for pacemaker – no drug maintenance.
- **Key interactions:** Atropine – enhances vagolytic effect; with other anticholinergics add toxicity.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Atropine is mainly metabolised by liver (CYP3A4) – no dose adjustment in mild impairment; caution in severe hepatic disease.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Atropine – giving a dose <0.5 mg IV can cause paradoxical bradycardia. Always give full 0.5 mg.

### Complications
- **Acute:** Hypotension, syncope, cardiac arrest (P-wave arrest, asystole), heart failure, myocardial ischaemia (in settings of low cardiac output)
- **Chronic:** Recurrent falls, fractures, cognitive decline; pacemaker complications (pneumothorax, lead displacement, infection)

### Prognosis
- **High-grade AV block (Mobitz II, 3rd degree) with wide QRS escape** – mortality 20–30% in first year without pacing; with permanent pacing, near-normal life expectancy if no structural heart disease.
- **Sinus node dysfunction** – good prognosis with pacing; symptoms resolve, but high risk of atrial arrhythmias later.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always get a 12-lead ECG** within minutes of bradycardia with symptoms – look for AV dissociation, P-wave axis, QRS width.
- **Apply pacing pads early** – even if you think atropine might work; prepare for deterioration.
- **Check the drug chart and TFT** – a simple medication review can prevent unnecessary pacing.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give atropine in narrow-angle glaucoma or high-grade AV block with wide QRS escape** – it is often ineffective and may worsen myocardial oxygen demand.
- **Do not delay transcutaneous pacing** in a patient with hypotension and wide QRS bradycardia – time lost worsens outcome.
- **Avoid verapamil or diltiazem** for atrial tachyarrhythmias if there is underlying bradycardia – they can cause complete heart block.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** New syncope, pre-syncope or blackouts; chest pain; severe dizziness or near-fainting; palpitations.
- **Follow-up:** Cardiology outpatient appointment in 2 weeks; 24-hour Holter or event monitor if e.g. sick sinus syndrome suspected; repeat ECG at follow-up.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / arrest team if:** HR <40 with hypotension (SBP <90) despite atropine; new wide QRS bradycardia; prolonged pause >5 s on monitor; syncope witnessed; NEWS2 ≥6 or any single red (e.g. resp rate ≤5, SpO2 <85, SBP <80).
- **Specific thresholds:** GCS <13, lactate >4 mmol/L, pH <7.2, hypotension (SBP <90) not corrected by initial atropine/pacing.
- **Always inform cardiology registrar** of any new high-grade AV block – they may need to come for transvenous pacing.
